comment_text.sn3.18:3.1: “Townsville” is a literal rendering of nagaraka. I translate it in homage to the town in northern Queensland where a chance encounter with a joey set me on the path to the Dhamma. The event the Buddha refers to here is recorded at SN 45.2.

root_text.sn3.18:1.2: Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho rājā pasenadi kosalo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn3.18:1.3: “idha mayhaṁ, bhante, rahogatassa paṭisallīnassa evaṁ cetaso parivitakko udapādi:
root_text.sn3.18:1.4: ‘svākkhāto bhagavatā dhammo, so ca kho kalyāṇamittassa kalyāṇasahāyassa kalyāṇasampavaṅkassa, no pāpamittassa no pāpasahāyassa no pāpasampavaṅkassā’”ti.
root_text.sn3.18:2.2: Svākkhāto, mahārāja, mayā dhammo. So ca kho kalyāṇamittassa kalyāṇasahāyassa kalyāṇasampavaṅkassa, no pāpamittassa no pāpasahāyassa no pāpasampavaṅkassāti.
root_text.sn3.18:3.1: Ekamidāhaṁ, mahārāja, samayaṁ sakkesu viharāmi nagarakaṁ nāma sakyānaṁ nigamo.
root_text.sn3.18:3.2: Atha kho, mahārāja, ānando bhikkhu yenāhaṁ tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā maṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho, mahārāja, ānando bhikkhu maṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn3.18:4.1: Evaṁ vuttāhaṁ, mahārāja, ānandaṁ bhikkhuṁ etadavocaṁ:
root_text.sn3.18:4.3: Sakalameva hidaṁ, ānanda, brahmacariyaṁ—yadidaṁ kalyāṇamittatā kalyāṇasahāyatā kalyāṇasampavaṅkatā.
root_text.sn3.18:4.4: Kalyāṇamittassetaṁ, ānanda, bhikkhuno pāṭikaṅkhaṁ kalyāṇasahāyassa kalyāṇasampavaṅkassa ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāvessati ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkarissati.
root_text.sn3.18:5.1: Kathañca, ānanda, bhikkhu kalyāṇamitto kalyāṇasahāyo kalyāṇasampavaṅko ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāveti, ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaroti?
root_text.sn3.18:5.2: Idhānanda, bhikkhu sammādiṭṭhiṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ, sammāsaṅkappaṁ bhāveti … sammāvācaṁ bhāveti … sammākammantaṁ bhāveti … sammāājīvaṁ bhāveti … sammāvāyāmaṁ bhāveti … sammāsatiṁ bhāveti … sammāsamādhiṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ.
root_text.sn3.18:5.3: Evaṁ kho, ānanda, bhikkhu kalyāṇamitto kalyāṇasahāyo kalyāṇasampavaṅko ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāveti, ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaroti.
root_text.sn3.18:5.4: Tadamināpetaṁ, ānanda, pariyāyena veditabbaṁ yathā sakalamevidaṁ brahmacariyaṁ—yadidaṁ kalyāṇamittatā kalyāṇasahāyatā kalyāṇasampavaṅkatāti.
root_text.sn3.18:6.1: Mamañhi, ānanda, kalyāṇamittaṁ āgamma jātidhammā sattā jātiyā parimuccanti, jarādhammā sattā jarāya parimuccanti, byādhidhammā sattā byādhito parimuccanti, maraṇadhammā sattā maraṇena parimuccanti, sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsadhammā sattā sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsehi parimuccanti.
root_text.sn3.18:6.2: Iminā kho etaṁ, ānanda, pariyāyena veditabbaṁ yathā sakalamevidaṁ brahmacariyaṁ—yadidaṁ kalyāṇamittatā kalyāṇasahāyatā kalyāṇasampavaṅkatā’ti.
root_text.sn3.18:8.1: Kalyāṇamittassa te, mahārāja, kalyāṇasahāyassa kalyāṇasampavaṅkassa ayaṁ eko dhammo upanissāya vihātabbo—
root_text.sn3.18:9.1: Appamattassa te, mahārāja, viharato appamādaṁ upanissāya, itthāgārassa anuyantassa evaṁ bhavissati:
root_text.sn3.18:9.3: Handa mayampi appamattā viharāma, appamādaṁ upanissāyā’ti.
root_text.sn3.18:10.1: Appamattassa te, mahārāja, viharato appamādaṁ upanissāya, khattiyānampi anuyantānaṁ evaṁ bhavissati:
root_text.sn3.18:10.3: Handa mayampi appamattā viharāma, appamādaṁ upanissāyā’ti.
root_text.sn3.18:11.1: Appamattassa te, mahārāja, viharato appamādaṁ upanissāya, balakāyassapi evaṁ bhavissati:
root_text.sn3.18:11.3: Handa mayampi appamattā viharāma, appamādaṁ upanissāyā’ti.
root_text.sn3.18:12.1: Appamattassa te, mahārāja, viharato appamādaṁ upanissāya, negamajānapadassapi evaṁ bhavissati:
root_text.sn3.18:12.3: Handa mayampi appamattā viharāma, appamādaṁ upanissāyā’ti?
root_text.sn3.18:13.1: Appamattassa te, mahārāja, viharato appamādaṁ upanissāya, attāpi gutto rakkhito bhavissati—
root_text.sn3.18:13.2: itthāgārampi guttaṁ rakkhitaṁ bhavissati, kosakoṭṭhāgārampi guttaṁ rakkhitaṁ bhavissatī”ti.

translation_text.sn3.18:1.2: Seated to one side, King Pasenadi said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn3.18:1.3: “Just now, sir, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind.
translation_text.sn3.18:1.4: ‘The teaching is well explained by the Buddha. But it’s for someone with good friends, companions, and associates, not for someone with bad friends, companions, and associates.’”
translation_text.sn3.18:2.1: “That’s so true, great king! That’s so true!” said the Buddha. And he repeated the king’s statement, adding:
translation_text.sn3.18:3.1: “Great king, this one time I was staying in the land of the Sakyans where they have a town named Townsville.
translation_text.sn3.18:3.2: Then the mendicant Ānanda came to me, bowed, sat down to one side, and said:
translation_text.sn3.18:3.3: ‘Sir, good friends, companions, and associates are half the spiritual life.’
translation_text.sn3.18:4.1: When he had spoken, I said to him:
translation_text.sn3.18:4.3: Good friends, companions, and associates are the whole of the spiritual life.
translation_text.sn3.18:4.4: A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path.
translation_text.sn3.18:5.1: And how does a mendicant with good friends develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path?
translation_text.sn3.18:5.2: It’s when a mendicant develops right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go.
translation_text.sn3.18:5.3: That’s how a mendicant with good friends develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path.
translation_text.sn3.18:5.4: And here’s another way to understand how good friends are the whole of the spiritual life.
translation_text.sn3.18:6.1: For, by relying on me as a good friend, sentient beings who are liable to rebirth, old age, and death, to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are freed from all these things.
translation_text.sn3.18:6.2: This is another way to understand how good friends are the whole of the spiritual life.’
translation_text.sn3.18:7.1: So, great king, you should train like this:
translation_text.sn3.18:7.2: ‘I will have good friends, companions, and associates.’
translation_text.sn3.18:8.1: When you have good friends, companions, and associates, you should live supported by one thing:
translation_text.sn3.18:9.1: When you’re diligent, supported by diligence, your ladies of the harem,
translation_text.sn3.18:12.1: and people of town and country will think:
translation_text.sn3.18:12.2: ‘The king lives diligently, supported by diligence.
translation_text.sn3.18:12.3: We’d better live diligently, supported by diligence!’
translation_text.sn3.18:13.1: When you’re diligent, supported by diligence, then not only you yourself,
translation_text.sn3.18:13.2: but your ladies of the harem, and your treasury and storehouses will be guarded and protected.”
translation_text.sn3.18:13.3: That is what the Buddha said. …
translation_text.sn3.18:15.4: they are said to be astute.”
